query: impact of early vs late surfactant administration on chronic lung disease and mortality in preterm infants

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Interstitial Emphysema -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of PIE varies depending on the population studied, such as patients with low birth weight, respiratory distress syndrome, or other etiologies. A study examined infants with respiratory distress syndrome who were born prematurely at less than 30 weeks. [11] A comparison of surfactant administration as prophylaxis versus early treatment in premature infants revealed a higher incidence of PIE in both premature infants and those who received late surfactant treatment. [12] [13] Premature infants born at 25 to 29 weeks of gestation who were randomly treated with surfactant at birth developed PIE in 15% of the treated infants and 26% of the control infants. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis of infants managed with antenatal steroids, respiratory support, and exogenous surfactant therapy is excellent. Mortality is less than 10%, with some studies showing survival rates of up to 98% with advanced care. Increased survival in developed countries is in stark comparison to babies who received no intervention in low-income countries, where the mortality rate for premature infants with RDS is significantly higher, at times close to 100%. [40] With adequate ventilatory support alone, surfactant production eventually begins, and once surfactant production begins along with the onset of diuresis, RDS improves within 4 or 5 days. Untreated disease leading to severe hypoxemia in the first days of life can result in multiple organ failure and death.

retrieved_docs.title: Surfactant -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary surfactant is a lipoprotein complex responsible for preventing the collapse of alveoli and increasing lung compliance. [1] Therapeutically,  exogenous surfactant administration, or surfactant replacement therapy(SRT), is considered in preterm infants with radiographic (small volume lungs, haziness/ ground-glass appearance, air bronchograms, and loss of cardiac borders on chest radiographs) and clinical evidence of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (NRDS). [2] Surfactant administration in preterm infants with NRDS reduces morbidity and mortality. There is a reduced risk of air leak and chronic lung disease. In NRDS, the earlier surfactant is used, the better the outcomes. Also, the outcome is reportedly better if early surfactant administration is followed by extubation of preterm infants with RDS compared to prolonged ventilation after surfactant administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Exogenous Surfactant Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: The targeted treatment for surfactant deficiency is intratracheal surfactant replacement therapy via an endotracheal tube. Surfactant administered within 30 to 60 minutes of the birth of a premature neonate is found to be beneficial. Surfactant hastens recovery and decreases the risk of pneumothorax, interstitial emphysema, intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH), BPD, and neonatal mortality in the hospital and at one year. However, neonates who receive surfactant for established RDS, have an increased risk of apnea of prematurity. According to European census guidelines, the surfactant is administered to immature babies with FiO2 > 0.3, and mature babies with FiO2 > 0.4. Currently, there are no clinically significant advantages of using one type over another when used in similar doses: [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Newborn -- Pathophysiology -- Pulmonary
retrieved_docs.content: It is well known that the adequate development of the fetal lung and surfactant secretion is an essential adaptation in preparation for birth and extrauterine life. From approximately weeks 22-28 of gestation, surfactant begins to be synthesized. Pulmonary surfactant is a composition of proteins and lipids found within the fluid lining the alveolar surface of the lungs. The primary role of surfactant is to prevent alveolar collapse at low lung volumes. [15] As delivery approaches, fetal lung fluid secretion ceases, causing a decrease in the volume of fetal lung fluid. The pulmonary pathophysiology of a newborn can be categorized based on timing and/or method of birth. Preterm infants, for example, have immature lungs that prove challenging to ventilate due to inadequate surfactant to decrease surface tension.

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Clinical Significance -- Impact on Invasive Ventilation
retrieved_docs.content: The use of NCPAP, like bCPAP, has been associated with a statistically significant reduction in the need for and duration of mechanical ventilation (the number needed to treat for additional benefit is equal to 6). [61] [67] [83] De Klerk et al demonstrated that bCPAP can reduce the number of infants ventilated and receiving surfactant and the mean duration of ventilator and oxygen therapy. [84] Moreover, bCPAP can reduce delivery room intubations, days on mechanical ventilation, and postnatal steroids. [85] Besides, a quality improvement initiative that implemented bCPAP showed that this respiratory support system is a potentially better practice among very low birth weight infants with RDS to reduce the need for mechanical ventilation. [83] Additionally, the effectiveness of bCPAP is also noted in animal studies; bCPAP was superior to conventional CPAP in maintaining airway patency, resulting in improved physiological outcomes in spontaneously breathing lambs with acute respiratory distress. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Surfactant -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary surfactant is a lipoprotein complex that lines the alveoli and decreases the surface tension to prevent lung atelectasis. Surfactant deficiency is a documented cause of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (NRDS), a significant cause of morbidity and mortality in premature infants. Therapeutic indications for surfactant replacement therapy include neonates with clinical and radiographic evidence of respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) and infants who require endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation secondary to respiratory failure. Surfactant is prophylactically administered in neonates at risk of developing NRDS. This activity will discuss the biochemical structure of surfactant, their administration, related complications, and the future of surfactant therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Assisted Ventilation of the Neonate
retrieved_docs.content: The goals of assisted ventilation are to reduce atelectasis by providing a constant distending positive airway pressure. The current preferred strategy is the early initiation of continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) with selective surfactant administration. [24] In most institutions, non-invasive modalities are preferred over invasive ventilation as they decrease the risk of mortality, and bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) compared to invasive ventilation with or without surfactant. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Compliance -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn respiratory distress syndrome: Surfactant secretion typically begins between the sixth and seventh months of gestation. Premature newborns may have little or no surfactant in their alveoli at birth, resulting in a significant tendency for their lungs to collapse. As mentioned earlier, surfactant reduces surface tension, thereby increasing lung compliance. The absence of surfactant leads to decreased pulmonary compliance, a condition known as newborn respiratory distress syndrome. This condition can be life-threatening and requires aggressive intervention, including continuous positive pressure breathing.

retrieved_docs.title: Surfactant -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic use of surfactant is indicated in neonates who are predisposed to the development of NRDS. These include those born before 32 weeks gestation and those with low birth weights (less than 1300 gm). In premature infants at risk for developing RDS, the surfactant administration with brief lung-protective ventilation (followed by extubation to nasal CPAP) has been found to lower the incidence of mechanical ventilation, air leak syndromes and result in a lower incidence of pneumothorax, pulmonary interstitial emphysema and improved survival without bronchopulmonary dysplasia. Prophylactic surfactant is also indicated in infants with laboratory evidence of surfactant deficiency (lecithin/sphingomyelin ratio of less than 2 to 1, evidence of lung immaturity on bubble stability test, or absence of phosphatidylglycerol). Surfactant replacement therapy may also be considered in other situations like worsening pulmonary hemorrhage. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Antenatal Management
retrieved_docs.content: Amnioinfusion Results from a study conducted by Locatelli et al in 49 patients with premature rupture of membranes at less than 26 weeks of gestation demonstrated that serial amnioinfusions reduce perinatal complications and prolong pregnancy. [40] Results from the amnioinfusion in preterm premature rupture of membranes (AMIPROM) pilot study conducted on 56 patients found no statistically significant differences in fetal or maternal outcomes. More extensive studies are needed to ascertain its benefits. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Complications -- Multidisciplinary Management to Mitigate Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Comprehensive care involving neonatologists, pulmonologists, cardiologists, and other specialists is critical. Early diagnosis, aggressive management of pulmonary hypertension, and optimization of nutritional and respiratory support are essential for improving outcomes and reducing the long-term burden of complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Interstitial Emphysema -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: PIE is more commonly observed in premature infants during the first few weeks of life. However, PIE can also occur in adults with conditions, such as asthma and barotrauma. Premature infants who develop PIE within 48 hours of birth typically have a poor prognosis. These infants may also have associated conditions such as low birth weight, premature birth, perinatal asphyxia, and sepsis. [9] Earlier studies found no significant difference in PIE incidence between sexes. [10] A retrospective study found that 24% of extremely premature infants on ventilators, who were treated with antenatal steroids, tocolysis, and postnatal surfactant, developed PIE later in their hospital course. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: High Frequency Ventilation -- Indications -- Neonates
retrieved_docs.content: Persistent pulmonary hypertension ARDS Pulmonary interstitial emphysema Meconium aspiration Pulmonary hypoplasia

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Clinical Significance -- Impact on Pulmonary Tissue and Airways
retrieved_docs.content: In preterm lambs, the application of bCPAP was associated with decreased alveolar structural damage, pulmonary edema, inflammation, and fibrosis. Additionally, a decrease in neutrophil migration and hydrogen peroxide production in bronchoalveolar lavage was demonstrated by the application of bCPAP. [89] Improved peripheral airway patency also resulted from bCPAP use, resulting in decreased lung inhomogeneity and reduced alveolar protein levels, suggesting a lesser degree of lung injury. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Indications -- Respiratory Distress in Spontaneously Breathing Neonate
retrieved_docs.content: Bubble CPAP is indicated for conditions that result in respiratory distress, eg, RDS of prematurity, chronic pulmonary insufficiency of prematurity, transitional tachypnea of the newborn (TTN), meconium aspiration syndrome (MAS), pulmonary hypertension, and congenital pneumonia, to name a few. For example, surfactant deficiency in RDS causes the collapse of the terminal airways, leading to ventilation-perfusion mismatch and increased work of breathing. By providing constant distending pressure and stability to the highly compliant chest wall of preterm neonates, bCPAP maintains lung volume at the end of expiration, reducing atelectasis and respiratory fatigue and eventually preventing respiratory failure. [17] [18] This is achieved even in a state of relative surfactant deficiency. Moreover, the oscillatory effect imposed by the bCPAP has been shown to enhance the natural release of surfactant in preterm infants with RDS. [19] [20] Thus, in 2022, the WHO recommended using bCPAP over ventilator-CPAP for respiratory support in preterm or low birth weight neonates (conditional recommendation, low-certainty evidence). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a hierarchical framework that links gestational age, growth parameters, perinatal infections, and neonatal complications. Completion of 29‑30 weeks of gestation is classified as a preterm (خديج) condition and is considered a liveborn outcome; “liveborn” functions as a broad category that contains newborn disorders (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة), which are a subclass of the preterm condition. Preterm birth is frequently associated with respiratory distress syndrome (متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة), also termed pulmonary surfactant deficiency syndrome, highlighting the causal link between early gestation and immature lung development. Respiratory distress syndrome is simultaneously classified as a respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) and a lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), and is a member of the neonatal‑and‑newborn disorder set (اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة, SMQ). In ICD‑10‑AM it maps to the code for اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ under the parent “Duration of gestation,” and within

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 533
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base describes a hierarchical taxonomy of perinatal and neonatal disorders that integrates gestational timing, birth weight, fetal growth, and specific clinical conditions across multiple languages and coding systems. A gestational period of 29–30 weeks is classified as خديج (preterm) and is a subtype of liveborn infant; خديج is causally linked to متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary surfactant deficiency syndrome) and to respiratory distress syndrome (RDS), which is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (newborn disorder). اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة is a member of the SMQ group اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة and is associated with عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك (unspecified perinatal infection). Transient neonatal diabetes (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL 1 and 2), developmental delay, epilepsy, and other neonatal metabolic disorders are also mapped to اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة. Disorders relating to short gestation and/or low birthweight are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) represent a broad category of early‑life health problems, and within this group the respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29) are classified as a child subgroup. This hierarchical relationship indicates that the latter are a subset of the broader perinatal condition category, emphasizing the need to consider these specific respiratory and cardiovascular issues when assessing and managing perinatal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1707
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) is a broad category of conditions affecting newborns. Respiratory distress syndrome is identified as a specific type of neonatal disorder, and the category also includes the child conditions fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية) and premature rupture of membranes (رضح الولادة). Prevention and control measures are applicable to the overall neonatal disorder as well as to each of its constituent conditions—respiratory distress syndrome, fetal erythroblastosis, and premature rupture of membranes—underscoring the importance of proactive management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 388
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period described as “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is classified under the Arabic term خديج (preterm birth) and is linked to a liveborn outcome. Preterm births (خديج) are frequently caused by متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). The concept of Duration of gestation is a key attribute of liveborn infants and is organized within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM). Within this classification system, respiratory disorders (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) are listed as a child category, reflecting the close relationship between gestational age, preterm status, underlying pulmonary immaturity, and respiratory complications. Recognizing these interconnections and their coding in ICD‑10‑AM supports accurate diagnosis, documentation, and clinical management of infants born at 29–30 weeks gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 943
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) are situated within the broader category of Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation, which is classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}. Within the perinatal‑originating conditions, there is a more specific subgroup of Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29). This hierarchical organization indicates that infants born with extreme prematurity and unspecified low birth weight are at risk for a range of perinatal conditions, including distinct respiratory and cardiovascular disorders, highlighting the importance of recognizing the underlying gestational and growth‑related factors when assessing and managing newborn health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

Overall, the network integrates gestational age, fetal growth and nutritional status, prematurity‑related lung immaturity, a spectrum of respiratory and cardiovascular complications, metabolic and infectious neonatal disorders, and traumatic perinatal events into a unified classification framework (ICD‑10‑AM, SMQ, and language‑specific groupings). This structure supports accurate diagnosis, coding, reporting, and targeted clinical management of infants born at risk for short gestation, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, perinatal infection, or birth‑related injury.
